data.title: U.S. Bancorp: A Solid Buy Case
data.description:
U.S. Bancorp (USB) sailed past consensus expectations for the fourth-quarter on Tuesday due to a robust performance in the bank's core lending and fee-generating businesses. The regional bank reported 2.3% year-over-year growth in its average loans as well as 3.3% year-over-year growth in its net interest income, a key measure that expresses the health of its lending operations. I like that the regional lender is expanding its loan operations, sees strong fee income growth and is managing to grow its book value per-share. As a result, I am confirming my buy rating for the regional banking powerhouse and see a positive investment case for U.S. Bancorp in 2026.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of U.S. Bancorp a buy in my last coverage in October -- Strong Growth Proposition -- shortly after the bank’s third-quarter earnings because I liked the bank’s trend in net interest income as well as the fact that the regional lender has been successful in expanding its loan portfolio consistently in the last year... which also held true for the fourth-quarter. Shares of U.S. Bancorp are priced at a ~50% premium to book value, but with strong asset growth in fee-related income streams, I believe the regional bank has potential for more upside in 2026.

U.S. Bancorp's Loan And NII Growth Are Solid, Highlighting A Strong Value Proposition For Investors In 2026

The lender beat top and bottom line estimates for its fourth fiscal quarter on Tuesday: U.S. Bancorp reported $1.26 per-share in GAAP earnings, beating the consensus estimate by $0.07 per-share. The revenue figure came in at $7.37B and was therefore $50M higher than the consensus estimate.

U.S. Bancorp’s total revenues reached $7.4B in Q4'25 and increased 5.1% year-over-year, in large part due to a growing loan portfolio and higher net interest income that continues to grow for the regional lender in a high-rate environment. The bank's total income surged a massive 23% year-over-year to $2.1B, highlighting strong operational performance in the last quarter of the year.

U.S. Bancorp’s net interest income in the last quarter reached $4.3B, which increased 3.3% year-over-year. Net interest income was, together with growing fee income, the biggest driver of the regional lender’s top-line growth in the fourth-quarter. The bank benefited from an improving deposit mix as well as fixed asset repricing and has positioned itself for more growth in 2026.

Current consensus interest rate expectations indicate that the Federal Reserve could lower interest rates one time each in 2026 and in 2027, at an average of 25 basis points, so the market essentially prices only two federal fund rate cuts in over the next two years. Faster falling interest rates, however, would pose a short-term risk to the bank's net interest income growth and may depress USB's share price going forward.

U.S. Bancorp continued to manage to grow its loans in the fourth-quarter as well which is critical to net interest income and total earnings growth. The average loan balance in Q4’25 was $384.3B, showing 2.3% Y/Y growth compared to the year-earlier period. This expansion in the loan portfolio was largely driven by growing total loan balances in the Commercial & Credit Card businesses which is where total outstanding loans grew to $180M in Q4'25 (+9.1%).

In addition to robust net interest income growth, U.S. Bancorp benefited from strong growth in fee-related income streams such as Capital Markets services, Investment Management and others... all of which help the bank become less reliant on the interest rate environment. U.S. Bancorp's fee-related income reached a total of $3.05B in Q4'25, showing 7.6% growth year-over-year. On a full-year basis, the bank's fee revenues, which are part of its non-interest income, reached $11.95B, showing 6.7% Y/Y growth.

The bank’s total net charge-off ratio -- which indicates overall asset quality -- was practically unchanged in the last quarter: it was published at 0.54% in Q4'25 vs. 0.56% in Q3'25 and 0.60% in Q4'24. Low net charge-off ratios and low credit provisions mean that the bank's loans don't send any distress signals which in turn indicates potential for loan growth. U.S. Bancorp's total credit provisions in Q4'25 were $577M, only about $17M higher than in the year-earlier period.

U.S. Bancorp's Valuation Vs. Other Regional Banks

U.S. Bancorp is currently valued at a ~50% premium to book value which is slightly higher than what regional banking peer such as PNC Financial (PNC) is trading at. PNC Financial, Truist (TFC) and Capital One (COF) are trading at price-to-book ratios between 1.07X and 1.49X. Compared to the 3-year historical P/B ratio of 1.32X, shares of USB are trading at a 13% premium to book value, but the bank has strong catalysts in its business, despite the growing risk of further federal fund rate cuts in 2026.

One catalyst is that the U.S. is growing rapidly, meaning the lender should see strong growth tailwinds in its core loan operations in 2026. Further, falling interest rates may lead to a drop-off in NII, but the bank's core loan portfolio could continue to grow. Thirdly, U.S. Bancorp is increasingly reliant on fee-related segment to drive its revenue growth, and fees are growing faster than other sources of income, such as net interest income. Lastly, USB is growing its book value at double-digit (+13% Y/Y) which makes the regional lender a growth play for bank investors.

Risks With U.S. Bancorp

As a regional banking powerhouse, U.S. Bancorp has considerable exposure to interest rates and since the Federal Reserve has started to lower interest rates again in December 2025, chances are that the Federal Reserve is going to continue to lower federal fund rates in 2026 potentially pressuring the bank's net interest income trajectory. However, the bank is increasingly broadly positioned and captures a growing share of income from non-interest related sources, like its fee-based segments in advisory and investment products. What would change my mind about U.S. Bancorp is if the lender were to see a broad-scale contraction in both NII and fee-driven income sources in 2026.

Final Thoughts

U.S. Bancorp submitted a strong earnings report card for the fourth fiscal quarter, which capped off a strong year for the bank as well as the regional banking market in general. Regional lenders have benefited greatly from the Federal Reserve’s slow pace in terms of lowering federal fund rates, which has supported NII gains. While the interest rate environment is set to become less advantageous for banks with large loan portfolios in 2026, the U.S. economy is growing and inflation is receding, creating tailwinds for higher consumer and business spending. U.S. Bancorp's loan portfolio is still growing and since the bank still benefits from strong asset quality and low loan provisions, I don’t see a reason to change my buy rating for USB.

data.title: D.R. Horton: Affordability Concerns Likely To Persist (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of D.R. Horton, Inc. (DHI) have been a modest performer over the past year, gaining about 6% in what has been a challenging environment for homebuilders. Hopes for a housing market turnaround in 2025 proved premature as long-term interest rates stayed elevated, compounding affordability challenges. More recently, with mortgage rates hitting 6%, hopes have built that 2026 could represent a genuine turn in the market. D.R. Horton is the nation’s leading homebuilder, and these challenges have weighed on results, though its strong balance sheet and industry-leading scale have helped DHI manage the downturn better than many. With DHI reporting Q1 results this morning, now is a good time to review shares. I recommend caution.

Incentives drive orders in Q1

In the company’s fiscal first quarter, D.R. Horton earned $2.03, which beat estimates by $0.11, even as revenue fell by 9%, given the weak environment for new home sales. During the quarter, DHI closed on 17,818 homes, down 7% from last year. That said, incentives remained elevated as DHI offers deals to entice buyers. Given increased incentives and some tariff costs, margins contracted by 260bps from last year to 23.8%. Given soft demand, DHI has maintained solid cost discipline with SG&A expenses down $13 million to $865 million, though they still rose as a share of revenue.

It was not all bad news because new orders were up 3% to 18,300. I was encouraged to see new orders not only rise but also exceed deliveries by 500, as a book-to-bill ratio above 1.0x is essential to return to sales growth. The cancellation rate was stable at 18%. Its backlog now stands at 11,376 units worth $4.3 billion, equivalent to about 2 months of sales. DHI has 30,400 homes in inventory, including 7,300 completed and unsold homes, so it will need a solid spring selling season to work through this inventory. Otherwise, we may see a need to further increase incentives. As you can see below, its backlog growth is concentrated in the North region, which has the best supply dynamics. While its backlog is up 3%, the value is up less than 0.5%, reflecting ongoing pressure on sales prices.

Key Sun Belt markets are soft

The housing market has been challenging for the homebuilders, as those who follow the sector know all too well. While home prices are up 0.1% nationally, there is significant geographic dispersion. As you can see below, many of the nation’s top 50 metro areas are underperforming the nation. These are predominantly in Florida, Texas, Arizona, and expansion markets inside California, as well as other Sun Belt locations. Conversely, prices in the Midwest and Northeast have held up better.

This dispersion is primarily due to the fact that there is more supply in these new markets, and builders like DHI are much more exposed. DHI gets the majority of its revenue in Southern regions, so while the national home price environment may be flat, if we weighted its regions, prices are likely down in the low-single digits. Indeed, DHI does not even operate in New England, where prices have been firm.

Market fundamentals are mixed

Now, while I do not expect a price surge in 2026, given affordability challenges, there are reasons to expect some stabilization in the market. As you can see below, 30-year mortgage rates are sitting at a 3-year low with rates right around 3%. President Trump’s push to get Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to buy $200 billion of mortgage-backed securities has helped to compress mortgage spreads, at least temporarily. That said, if long-term treasury yields move higher (as they have recently), that will increase mortgage rates. Ultimately, I believe we will need to see the United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) no more than 4% for mortgage rates to sustainably breach 6%. That said, if rates can remain in the 6-6.25% area for the next few months, that should make for a better spring selling season with some buyers likely to enter the market.

Importantly, the supply picture has also improved as builders have pulled back construction amid weak demand. For the first time since 2020, there are fewer than 600,000 single-family homes under construction. This is down about 28% from its peak, and I expect further declines at least into Q3, given slow housing starts. All else equal, diminishing supply should support prices. Still, I would note that the amount of supply is substantially above pre-COVID levels. I view the supply picture as more of a lessening headwind than a genuine tailwind for new home prices.

DHI’s buyers are more affordability-exposed than buyers overall, as 63% of its customers are first-time buyers, meaning they do not have equity in a home that they can roll over to finance a down payment. Indeed, their average down payment is just 10%, meaning they typically rely on private mortgage insurance, further increasing the cost of the loan. Given these pressures, DHI has been shifting down market by significantly cutting the size of its homes to address affordability challenges. At $365k, its homes sell for about 27% less than the national average.

While lower mortgage rates are a clear benefit, I expect affordability challenges among first-time buyers to limit potential upside in sales activity. I see DHI’s business as being more exposed to ongoing softness than higher-end builders like Toll Brothers, Inc. (TOL).

Shares are priced in a market recovery that may not occur

Alongside results, D.R. Horton reaffirmed guidance for $33.5-$35 billion in revenue on 86-88,000 home sales. DHI is still targeting $2.5 billion of buybacks in the fiscal year. In keeping with this, DHI repurchased $670 million of stock in the quarter. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 9% from last year. Aside from buybacks, shares offer a 1.1% dividend yield, and I expect a ~6.5-7% capital return yield this year.

DHI has a solid balance sheet with a 19% debt-to-capital ratio, and it has $600 million of maturities over the next year, which it can easily refinance. This solid balance sheet allows DHI to focus on capital returns. Separately, I would note that D.R. Horton owns 62% of Forestar Group Inc. (FOR), which owns lots and reduces DHI’s capital intensity. Nearly a quarter of its lot purchases are from Forestar. This stake is worth about $860 million, or about $3/share. Given the integration of the two businesses, I do not expect DHI to monetize this stake.

Given my view, 6% is likely to be a floor on mortgage rates (and the recent tick-up in 10-year treasury yields adds to this belief), I expect deliveries to be in the lower half of guidance, and I am cautious on the spring selling season. On balance, I expect incentives to remain higher for longer, which will continue to exert downward pressure on DHI’s gross margins. That said, its scale and balance sheet position it well to endure ongoing market softness.

As a result, I see DHI earning $11.05-$11.35 this year, which is about 13.75x earnings or about 13.5x excluding its Forestar stake. For a cyclical business unlikely to see a sharp rebound in demand, this is a full multiple, and I struggle to value the homebuilders above 12x, given my caution on Sun Belt home prices. At this valuation, shares are already pricing in meaningful housing market improvement. I see fair value as no higher than $138, and any softness in the Spring selling season will likely push shares lower. As such, I rate DHI a Sell as the housing market is unlikely to have hit bottom, even as declining supply should slow the pace of deterioration.

data.title: CyberScope Web3 Security Aims For IPO Despite Dropping Revenue
data.description:
CyberScope’s Revenue Is Falling In A Growing Industry

CyberScope Web3 Security Inc. (CYSC) has filed to raise growth capital in an IPO of its ordinary shares, according to SEC F-1/A registration information.

CYSC provides blockchain-related security software solutions for decentralized systems.

The firm’s revenue is dropping sharply, it faces numerous risks, and its IPO valuation assumptions are excessive to the extreme, so my outlook is to sell (avoid) the CYSC IPO.

What Does CyberScope Do?

George Town, Cayman Islands-based CyberScope Web3 Security Inc. was founded in Greece, and offers various Web3 security tools and services for blockchain software systems.

Management is led by Chairman Mr. Trishneet Arora, who wholly owns KYE9 Artificial Intelligence Research, owner of 40% of CYSC’s stock.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Smart contract audits

  • KYC verification

  • Bug bounty program management

  • Penetration testing

  • Proprietary security tools

  • Other services.

CyberScope makes money primarily through selling its project-based auditing services for specialized security assessments.

CyberScope has booked fair market value investment of $2.3 million through June 30, 2025, from investors, including TAC Infosec Limited and KYE9 Artificial Intelligence Research LLC.

Sales and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen to essentially 0 as revenues have decreased, as the figures below indicate:

Sales and Marketing

Expenses vs. Revenue

Period

Percentage

Six Months Ended June 30, 2025

0.0%

Year Ended December 31, 2024

2.3%

February 8 to December 31, 2023

3.5%

(Source - SEC.)

The Rule of 40 is a software industry metric that says that if the combined revenue growth rate and operating margin equal or exceed 40%, the company is producing an acceptable growth/operating margin trajectory. (Operating margin strips out non-cash charges like SBC.)

CYSC’s most recent calculation was 46% as of June 30, 2025, and the company has achieved this via its high adjusted operating margin on falling revenue, per the table below:

Rule of 40

Calculation

Recent Rev. Growth %

-24%

Adjusted Operating Margin

69%

Total

46%

(Source - SEC.)

What Is CyberScope’s Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by Mordor Intelligence, the global market for block-based security solutions was an estimated $3 billion in 2025 and is expected to exceed $15 billion by 2030.

This represents a projected CAGR of 38.4% from 2026 to 2030, a very high expected rate of growth.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are enterprise migration toward Web3 approaches, growing incidence of smart-contract threats and exploits and greater "regulations that demand tamper-proof audit trails."

Also, elements that can constrain the industry's growth include difficulty in finding qualified blockchain security engineering personnel, regulatory uncertainties and the high cost of continuous code audits.

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

  • IBM (IBM)

  • Guardtime

  • Microsoft (MSFT)

  • CertiK Ltd.

  • Quantstamp

  • Consensys Diligence

  • Hacken

  • Halborn

  • Others.

The blockchain security industry appears to be shifting from a narrow code review mode to a more expansive approach to the entire operational lifecycle of a protocol.

It is also seeing growing demand for continuous real-time monitoring and AI-augmented verification technologies.

The firm sells its services via direct sales and marketing efforts to blockchain project owners and developers worldwide.

The company says it has completed more than 2,700 smart contract audits and over 500 KYC verifications with clients and partners such as ApeSwap, Quarashi Network and Coinscope, among many others.

Below is a thumbnail SWOT analysis for CyberScope:

Strengths

  • Proprietary smart contract analysis and compliance tools, audit certificate industry recognition, comprehensive service offering.

Weaknesses

  • Reliance on scarce technical talent, limited operating history, cost of keeping up with technological change.

Opportunities

  • Growing adoption of Web3, DeFi and tokenized assets, regulatory developments like MiCA increasing demand, strategic partnerships, investment in proprietary tools.

Threats

  • Cryptocurrency market volatility, intense competition from larger firms, project failure could lead to reputational damage.

CyberScope’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Declining top line revenue

  • Falling gross profit and gross margin

  • Dropping operating profit

  • A swing to cash used in operations.

Below are the major financial results from the firm’s current registration statement:

Total Revenue

(Source - SEC.)

CyberScope’s IPO Plan

CYSCY intends to sell one million shares of ordinary shares at a proposed midpoint price of $5.00 per share for gross proceeds of approximately $5.0 m, not including the sale of customary underwriter options.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares, according to the most recent IPO filing.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the normal and customary 180 days after the effective date of the IPO prospectus.

According to CyberScope’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

We intend to use the net proceeds from this offering for general corporate purposes, including sales and marketing expenditures aimed at growing our business and research and development expenditures focused on product development. Although we have not allocated specific portions of the net proceeds to specific uses, we expect that a significant portion of such expenditures will relate to hiring additional sales and marketing and research and development personnel. We may also use net proceeds from this offering to make acquisitions or investments in complementary companies or technologies, although we do not have any agreement or understanding with respect to any such acquisition or investment at this time.

(Source - SEC.)

The company’s enterprise value at IPO (assuming no underwriter options) will approximate $101 million.

Dilution in net tangible book value per share for investors in the IPO is expected to be $4.79.

The float-to-outstanding-shares ratio (excluding underwriter options) will be approximately 4.76%, so the stock will be an extremely ‘low float’ stock subject to higher volatility in post-IPO trading.

The firm’s presentation for its roadshow is not available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, the firm said it was not a party to any material legal actions as of the prospectus date (January 13, 2026).

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Maxim Group LLC.

Here is a table of relevant capitalization and valuation figures for the firm:

Measure [TTM]

Amount

Market Capitalization at IPO

$105,000,000

$100,867,685

85.47

(Glossary Of Terms)

(Source - SEC.)

As of June 30, 2025, CyberScope had $682,553 in cash and equivalents and $508,500 in total liabilities.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended June 30, 2025, of $24,830.

CyberScope’s Revenue Is Falling In A Growing Industry

CYCS aims to raise public capital investment to fund its general growth plans.

The company’s financials have produced falling topline revenue from a small base, declining gross profit and gross margin, reduced operating profit and a swing to cash used in operations.

Sales and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have dropped to zero, as it appears the company has spent almost nothing on this function.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends and will retain all available funds for its growth initiatives and working capital needs.

CYSC’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent almost zero on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

The company’s Rule of 40 results have been unusual, with a large drop in revenue more than offset by a high operating margin, contributing to a positive figure for this metric.

The firm’s growth strategy includes expanding its service offering to monitoring and incident response, talent acquisition and retention efforts and ongoing investment in R&D

The market opportunity for providing Web3 security services is moderate but expected to grow at a very high rate of growth in the coming years, so the company enjoys strong growth dynamics in its favor.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its "emerging growth company" and "foreign private issuer" status, which allows management to produce substantially less information for shareholders.

Micro-cap company stocks like these have frequently performed poorly in open market trading after their IPO.

The firm has revenue concentration risks in a relatively small part of the blockchain industry and it is highly dependent on a relatively limited number of knowledgeable blockchain code auditors.

CYCS’s related party transactions are detailed here.

As to valuation, CyberScope is asking investors to pay an EV/Revenue multiple of over 82x on sharply falling revenue from a small base, which is wildly excessive.

Given the numerous risks, worsening financial results and excessive valuation assumptions by management, my outlook is to sell (avoid) the CYCS IPO.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Zeta: Set To Surprise Investors, Here's Why
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Zeta (ZETA) has failed to catch any sort of bid for months. Naturally, without any sort of ''action'' a lot of investors have thrown in the towel, bored, and moved on to more exciting areas of the market.

However, I argue that in the market, the reward rarely goes to the impatient. And what's more, I see Zeta's second half of 2026 coming out particularly strongly, as its OpenAI collaboration starts to gather steam.

The appeal of this thesis is simple to grasp. In 2026, Zeta's revenue growth rates will naturally accelerate, given the way its comparable quarters lie. On top of that, Zeta's OpenAI collaboration should start to accelerate in the coming months, thus not only strengthening Zeta's position in AI-driven marketing but provides Zeta with a fresh narrative.

Yes, there are some pesky risk factors that we have to be aware of, which I openly discuss. But I believe that paying just 22x forward free cash flow has already priced in too much negativity.

Here's why I'm bullish.

Zeta's Embrace of OpenAI

Over the past few months, ZETA's stock has gone nowhere fast, even as I've been openly bullish on the name; read more on my previous analysis here.

There has been a lot of sideways movement, with many companies outside the mega-cap names drifting lower, which is a pattern that tends to repeat from time to time.

This has been most noticeable in software names, where the question of AI replacing the overall need to pay heavy fees for software companies has been brought into question.

Meanwhile, I argue that Zeta is not in that bucket. Instead, Zeta uses data models to target advertising, and as such is not in the crosshairs of potentially being displaced by AI.

Furthermore, critical evidence that AI is not disrupting Zeta, but going to benefit its prospects, has been the recent tie-up with OpenAI, announced in January.

In simple terms, Zeta plans to roll out its Athena product to all customers by the end of the first quarter of 2026.

As a reminder, Zeta is an all-in-one platform for marketers. Furthermore, through its platform, users can plan and analyze marketing campaigns simply by describing their objectives in plain spoken English.

Again, I argue that Zeta's key advantage is that

As the OpenAI partnership becomes more deeply embedded within Zeta's Athena platfrom, it will be easier for customers to turn their raw data into actionable insights, but that AI will displace the need for Zeta's marketing platforms, and other adtech companies, is in my opinion ludicrious.

Zeta benefits from the tie-up with OpenAI, it's not disrupted by OpenAI.

With this framing in mind, let's discuss Zeta's guidance for 2026.

2026 Organic Growth Rates of 20%+

There are two aspects that make me believe Zeta's 2026 performance is likely to be better than what the market is currently expecting.

The most significant of these is the already discussed OpenAI partnership.

At the same time, a positive narrative only holds up if it is backed by solid business performance. The other aspect that I believe will propel Zeta forward in 2026 is its large acquisition of Marigold.

As Zeta works toward its already-guided 35% y/y revenue growth and a target of $1.7 billion in sales, investors are likely to see both improving fundamentals and a more exciting story. In short, a much more compelling investment case.

The market will view Zeta as a ''reaccelerating'' growth story. And for that, the market will pay a premium multiple.

ZETA Stock Valuation - 22x Forward Free Cash Flow

Before I get too optimistic about Zeta, it's important to acknowledge one significant concern that overhangs Zeta, namely its balance sheet. Right now, Zeta sits in a roughly net-neutral cash position, meaning it doesn't have a large cash buffer, which is a clear limitation at present and likely to overhang the stock for a little while.

Nevertheless, I expect Zeta to generate at least $225 million in free cash flow in 2026.

This means that as 2026 gathers steam, Zeta should start to gain some traction and noticeably improve its balance sheet, alongside a new and exciting narrative around its reaccelerating revenue growth. Together, these elements will substantially support a re-rating on ZETA's stock in the coming twelve months.

All in all, the stock trades at about 22 times forward free cash flow. For context, this implies an adjusted PEG ratio of 1x when swapping free cash flow for earnings. As a point of reference, I typically find anything below 3x attractive, so this valuation looks very compelling to me, thus offering an attractive risk-reward.

Keep These Risk Factors in Mind

The key risk in this thesis is whether Zeta's 2026 narrative is supported by fundamentals. Can Zeta provide investors with confidence that at least 20% of organic growth rates are on the cards in 2026?

While I don't believe this is a big risk factor, I have to allow for the possibility that OpenAI's tie-up ends up somehow eroding Zeta's pricing power. For that reason, I would recommend that we exit this investment if Zeta's free cash flow looks likely to come in below $180 million this year, for whatever reason.

Lastly, I know that AI is a rapidly moving technology. This could mean that there's the potential for a brand new AI player to emerge that argues that its revolutionizing the advertising sector.

As a matter of fact, in my experience, adtech companies are always so volatile, not only highly sensitive to their sector and broader economic factors, but also to the never-ending knife-edge competition amongst each other.

The Bottom Line

Zeta has been dull stock. As such, plenty of investors have understandably checked out.

If you are having fun, you are rarely making any money. Making money is often plagued by long periods of boredom.

Furthermore, boredom is often what precedes a move.

Plus, with revenues set to naturally reaccelerate in 2026 and a powerful new OpenAI-driven narrative coming into focus, the setup is quietly improving just as the investors are least excited about Zeta.

On top of that, expectations of strong free cash flow when taken together with a modest multiple give Zeta's stock tangible and underappreciated fundamentals, not just a story.

In sum, there's a razor-thin line between being stubborn and being patient, and to me, this is now the moment where patience is about to be rewarded.

data.title: Why Silver Continues To Be A Must Buy (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Even at close to $100 an ounce today, silver is still a must-buy. Here is why:

1. Silver vs The Quantity of US Dollars

Buying silver today with US dollars is a lot like buying silver in 1972. Below is a chart that shows the silver price relative to the monetary base (currency in circulation plus reserve balances):

When buying an ounce of silver today, you are using the same amount of US dollars relative to the total amount issued (according to the Fed) as you would have circa 1972.

Only at around $1630 per ounce (today) would silver be priced at the 1980 high, relative to total US dollars issued.

2. Silver vs Dow

Since the creation of the Federal Reserve, the Dow Jones has significantly outperformed silver.

It was only during brief periods before the 1919 and 1980 silver peaks that silver was able to outperform the Dow (see the Dow/Silver chart below).

The Dow/Silver ratio needs to rise above the double blue line in order to reach parity with the Dow since the creation of the Federal Reserve. At current prices, that would mean silver’s price needs to be way above $300.

Note that the Fed, by way of its money creation, first benefits the stock market, and then only at the end of that cycle do assets like silver and gold catch up and ultimately outperform (more detail here).

We are now in that catch-up phase. Relative to the Dow, silver is still historically very cheap. When faced with the option of buying either silver or the Dow today, it is a lot like 1972. Relative to the Dow’s valuation, you are paying the same as in 1972 for an ounce of silver.

Only when priced at $2785 per ounce would silver be priced at the 1980 high in terms of the Dow.

3. Still relatively early in this bull market

The current setup on the silver chart is a lot like the start of the silver bull market of the early 2000s. In those days, silver was under $6 and considered cheap.

The current bottoming pattern from around 2014 to now is very similar to the early 2000s bottoming pattern (see the chart below).

Find more detail on the chart here. I have indicated how the current chart position is similar to circa 2004, when prices were still around $6. 2004 was still very early in the bull market, an excellent time to buy. Buying at the current levels (given the above) presents equally great value.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SPEU: A Buying Opportunity Emerges For Dividend Growth Investors
data.description:
Introduction

Geopolitical tensions surrounding Greenland have contributed to a small pullback in European shares tracked by the SPDR Portfolio Europe ETF (SPEU). While I am skeptical of attributing all the decline to recent rhetoric by President Trump for acquiring the autonomous Danish territory, I do agree that this is what started the recent correction.

I would view the ongoing decline in European equities through the lens of other developments as well. Until recently there were hopes that a ceasefire in Ukraine would be achieved in the near future. Progress on that front has unfortunately been very limited, as evidenced by the recent consolidation of the EUR/USD exchange rate. Energy prices, a key European import, have also found a floor. The combined effect is that the prospect of ECB rate cuts (driven by a higher euro/lower energy prices) has receded.

Nevertheless, I believe these developments are largely priced in the undemanding valuation of European equities. As such, I confirm my previous Buy rating on SPEU, driven by:

  • A forward P/E of only 16.5x, implying an earnings yield roughly 2% higher than the S&P 500.
  • A trailing dividend yield of 3.40%, with my calculations showing dividend growth of 5.8% over the next decade.
  • Limited exposure to the information technology sector, notorious for low dividend payouts, making SPEU an ideal pick for dividend growth investors.

In this article, I will go over SPEU's basic characteristics, discuss its sector allocation, current valuations, dividend details, and risks to the SPEU investment case.

ETF Overview

You can access all SPEU results on the State Street website here. SPEU's portfolio currently totals 1,845 individual holdings, focused on developed European markets. Large ETF exposures include the U.K. (22.51% of ETF assets), France (15.15%), Switzerland (13.73%), and Germany (13.66%).

Despite holding just $0.75 billion in assets under management, SPEU has a paltry expense ratio of 0.07%, making it an ideal pick for long-term passive investors. Indeed, when discussing ETFs with friends, I always advise them to go for ETFs with fees of 0.1-0.2%.

Sector Allocation

SPEU's current sector allocation includes a heavy focus on cyclical sectors such as financials (24.08% of ETF assets), industrials (20.26%), consumer discretionary (8.02%), materials (5.48%), and energy (4.14%). These five sectors combined account for almost 62% of ETF assets, implying that SPEU should do well as long as we see solid GDP growth coming out of European countries where the ETF invests.

Relative to the S&P 500, a key underweight sector position is information technology (allocation of 7.64%), giving the ETF a smaller exposure to the AI growth theme.

The ETF's defensive sector positioning includes health care (13.27% of ETF assets), consumer staples (8.07%), utilities (4.41%), communication services (3.03%), and real estate (1.59%). Together these defensive sectors account for roughly 30% of ETF assets.

Current Valuation

While markets are quite volatile as I write this in mid-January 2026, I calculate that SPEU holdings trade at roughly 16.5x their forward earnings (earnings yield of about 6.06%). This is notably cheaper than the S&P 500, which trades at about 24.4x its forward earnings (earnings yield of about 4.1%).

In essence, current market pricing suggests that S&P 500 earnings growth would have to outpace SPEU earnings growth by roughly 2% in perpetuity. While I agree that the U.S. is likely to deliver GDP growth that is roughly 1% higher than that of most European countries, the discount offered by SPEU is twice that. Hence, on a net basis, SPEU looks like the better bet at the moment.

As such, if you are comfortable with SPEU's cyclical tilt (which will result in elevated volatility and more downside during recessions), I believe that from a fundamental perspective, you should have little issue picking SPEU over the S&P 500.

Dividend Details

SPEU's dividend yield stands at 3.40%, marginally higher than the 2.92% dividend yield of the underlying index. The difference between the two measures likely stems from recent USD weakness, as SPEU pays out dividends in USD but receives them in local European currencies.

SPEU's notably higher dividend relative to the S&P 500 partially stems from its underweight position in information technology stocks, notorious for their low dividend payouts.

SPEU has grown dividends by 4.60% annually over the past decade. Given current valuations and taking into account the ETF's small 0.07% expense ratio, I estimate that dividend growth should average about 5.8% over the next decade, driven by significant retained earnings at SPEU holdings.

Geopolitical considerations

So far we have seen that SPEU holdings offer attractive valuations and robust dividend prospects as long as the European economy holds up. This will largely depend on no sizable deterioration on the geopolitical front, most notably the war in Ukraine, but also tensions with the United States surrounding Greenland and trade policy.

The truth is that after the recent appreciation of the EUR relative to the USD, the United States is a low-margin market for European exporters, while U.S. exports to Europe have become increasingly lucrative for U.S. firms. As such, I don't expect an all-out trade war over Greenland. Instead, a deal enough to make President Trump happy while awaiting a new U.S. president in 2029 is my base case for the future of U.S.-Europe relations.

SPEU Rating

I still believe that SPEU offers good value for buy-and-hold investors who can look through near-term financial market volatility, as well as the inherent earnings unpredictability given SPEU's circa 62% exposure to cyclical sectors.

As such, I would view the current pullback in SPEU as a buying opportunity, confirming my Buy rating on the ETF. SPEU is particularly interesting for dividend growth investors, with a high, single-digit total return achievable over the next decade.

Near-term catalysts for SPEU may include a ceasefire in Ukraine/deal for Greenland. A return to the downtrend in energy prices, although unlikely in light of the broad-based strength in commodities, may also act as a catalyst for SPEU given Europe's reliance on energy imports.

Risks

Notable risks to the SPEU investment case include:

  • Downside surprises in European GDP growth, which will weigh on the earnings of cyclical SPEU holdings.
  • A larger-than-expected escalation in geopolitical tensions, principally in Ukraine but also in Greenland.
  • A reversal of recent USD weakness, which may materialize if we see a spike in energy prices/slower than expected Fed rate cuts.

Conclusion

SPEU is down a few percent as I write this, impacted by tensions over Greenland, no tangible progress on a ceasefire in Ukraine, and a stabilization in energy prices since the start of 2026.

I see the pullback as a buying opportunity, confirming my previous Buy rating on SPEU. The ETF's holdings offer a forward P/E of 16.5x and a total return potential in the high single digits, driven by an attractive 3.40% dividend yield and dividend growth of about 5.8% over the next decade.

Key risks to the SPEU investment case include downside surprises in European GDP growth, no de-escalation in geopolitical tensions, and a reversal of recent USD weakness.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: D.R. Horton: December Earnings Support Buying On Falling Mortgage Rates
data.description:
D.R. Horton (DHI) shares are up 2% in pre-market hours despite a broad market selloff, after the largest homebuilder in the United States reported better-than-expected results for the December quarter.

As Trump's administration directs attention towards affordability and lower rates, D.R. Horton is a great way to capitalize on declining mortgage rates.

In my view, this remains an opportune time to initiate a position in DHI, which is why I reiterate a "Buy."

Introduction To D.R. Horton, America's Largest Homebuilder

Founded in 1978, D.R. Horton grew to become America's largest seller of homes in terms of volume, with more than 1.2 million homes delivered since. It operates in 126 markets, and 36 states across the United States, making it the most diversified builder as well.

At an average sale price of $370,000, and 63% of customers being first-time homebuyers, it's clear who are Horton's customers, and what segment of the population it mostly appeals to.

We're talking about relatively young families, with 40 being the average age of the primary borrower and $96,000 the average household income. In other words, D.R. Horton sells homes to customers with inelastic demand. It's all about their ability to afford it.

This means that Horton's business is highly correlated with the macro environment, and specifically mortgage rates. Unsurprisingly, it has also been taking share consistently over the past 30 years.

Simply put, when the average household in America can afford to buy a home, D.R. Horton benefits.

Mortgage Rates At Three-Year Lows

The thing is, the average household in America couldn't really afford to buy a home in the past few years.

The National Association of Realtors publishes an affordability index that considers the median single-family home price, the average mortgage rate, and the median family income. When the index is above 100, it means the median family can afford a home, and below 100, vice versa.

As we can see, we've been around or below the 100 mark for the vast majority of the past three years, signaling the median family can barely afford a home. However, the last reading, in November, was the highest since 2022, and shows that we're making progress towards better affordability.

Of note, this improvement is mainly driven by declining mortgage rates, which stood at 6.3% at the last reading, and continued dropping since. As of today, we are at 6.06%, a three-year low:

President Trump's ordering Fannie and Freddie (FNMA) to buy $200 billion in mortgage bonds should support continued declines as well.

So, while affordability started improving in August, we're at a much better place today, and the recently announced results are yet to reflect that fully.

Horton Earnings Were Fine, But Are Yet To Reflect Better Macro

In Horton's fiscal Q1-26, consensus expected to see revenue of $6.65 billion, and EPS of $1.92. Horton exceeded expectations, with revenues of $6.9 billion, and earnings per share of $2.03.

Amid macro pressures, revenue was down 10% Y/Y, led by a 9% decline in homebuilding revenue, as they've closed ~1,200 less homes this quarter compared to last year. Homebuilding gross margins dropped from 22.5% to 20.2%, as DHI intentionally taps incentives and discounts to drive sales.

Homebuilding pre-tax income was down 30% Y/Y, given operational deleverage, and return on inventory dropped to 18.6%. However, we're seeing the company's improved business model in full force, as homebuilding operating cash flow was still strong at close to $500 million.

Furthermore, cancellation rates remained stable at 18%, showcasing DHI's competitive advantage as a diversified homebuilder that's selling to customers who are looking to live in the home rather than investors.

Non-homebuilding segments, which aren't that critical to the investment thesis, showed overall stability. The only outlier was the Rental segment, which experienced a sharper drop.

For now, Horton didn't upgrade its FY26 guidance. However, I strongly believe that they are being cautious, waiting to see if the recent positive developments around rates are here to stay. If they are, I think that a guidance upgrade in the next quarter is highly likely.

Valuation

At $156 per share, Horton trades at 14 times forward earnings, and 12 times next year's estimates.

Indeed, these are not trough multiples for a homebuilder, as we can see on the graph above. Broadly, Horton trades in the 8x-14x range.

However, there's a critical mechanism to understand with a cyclical stock like D.R. Horton. As a rule of thumb, you want to buy the stock when the multiple is "artificially" high on low-cycle earnings, and sell when the multiple is high on up-cycle earnings.

What do I mean by that?

Horton is expected to generate $13.1 in EPS in FY27, and $16.0 in EPS in FY28. In the previous up-cycle, Horton's EPS was as high as $16.7, and even in FY24, it was $14.4.

With Horton consistently taking market share, I think it's reasonable to expect that in the next good cycle, EPS will be at least $16.5, but analyst estimates won't reflect that until we actually experience such change.

Meanwhile, the valuation multiple might seem high, but if we used some level of normalized earnings, then Horton is actually trading at a single-digit P/E. And, this is precisely when there is the most upside with a stock like that.

Conclusion

D.R. Horton is arguably the best play on improving housing affordability, a trend that is only in motion, as reflected by lower mortgage rates and a rising affordability index.

Results are yet to reflect the macro improvement, and neither does the stock. In my view, this is an opportune time to initiate a position for investors who are looking to maximize the upside on the next positive housing cycle.

As such, I reiterate D.R. Horton at a "Buy."

data.title: Ionis: Expected 2026 Launch Of Olezarsen In SHTG To Spur Leap In Revenue Growth
data.description:
This is my lucky 13th article on Ionis (IONS), following my 09/2025 article — "Ionis: Recent Approvals, Upcoming Catalysts To Spur Strong Upside (Rating Upgrade)". It has been thumbs up for Ionis since this last article as it has risen ~20%.

In this article I will focus especially on Ionis' wholly owned Olezarsen, branded as TRYNGOLZA; it was approved by the FDA on 12/19/2024 used with diet, to reduce triglycerides [TG] in adults with familial chylomicronemia syndrome [FCS]. FCS is a small market subset of people with sHTG, those having extremely high TG's >880 mg/dL. FCS afflicts ~3,000 people in the US.

Healthy TG levels for adults are <150 mg/dL. As stated in slide 7 of its 01/2026 Presentation at the 2026 44th Annual J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference, sHTG is considered at play for those ~1 million Americans with fasting triglyceride levels ≥500 mg/dL. This is the market that Ionis guides as potentially generating $2 billion in revenues for TRYNGOLZA, if it is FDA approved to treat sHTG.

Product revenues are set to grow exponentially as a percentage of Ionis' complex revenue matrix.

Ionis' latest available 10-Q for Q3/2025 lists the following revenues for 3 and 9 months ended 9/30/2025:

Of its Q3/2025 ~$157 million in total revenues, only ~$32 million represents product revenues; of its ~$740 million in nine month 2025 revenues reported as of 9/30/2025 only ~$57 million represent product revenues.

This is a miserable effort for a company that was founded in 1989 and went public in 1991. I missed a big chunk of this pain. I began my coverage in 04/2018 on an optimistic note. At the time its SPINRAZA which it had dealt to Biogen (BIIB) was its top revenue source generating aggregate milestone revenues of $118 million.

Ionis had total revenues of >$500 million for 2017 with no product revenues. Some ~10 years later, in terms of product revenues the picture looks poised to change dramatically. Its full year 2025, TRYNGOLZA revenues in treatment of FCS are shown at $105 million in Presentation slide 8.

It has yet to report its revenues from its DAWNZERA (donidalorsen) approved by the FDA for treatment of Hereditary Angioedema [HAE] Prophylaxis in 08/2025. HAE represents the epitome of a crowded drug landscape as shown by the following graphic from BioCryst's (BCRX) Q3/2025 earnings presentation.

BioCryst's lead therapy is ORLADEYO in treatment of HAE shown as the bottom pale green bar on the chart below.

This graphic shows DAWNZERA revenues in brownish orange growing slowly from minuscule levels in 2025 to unspecified greater levels. It pegs DAWNZERA as never destined to reach a position with the top 3 or 4 HAE revenue generators.

Of course the chart reflects BioCryst's estimate; Ionis for its part is quite a lot more optimistic about Dawnzera's revenue potential. Interestingly, Ionis cites its study reporting that patients prefer DAWNZERA over both:

  1. Takedo's (TAK) TAKHZYRO (lanadelumab-flyo) the long time percentage leader in HAE shown as the second to bottom dark blue bar on the chart and
  2. BioCryst's ORLADEYO (berotralstat) pale green bottom bar on the chart.

The HAE battle is on; it will be in interesting to see how it plays out in the market over the next several years. Will Ionis' upstart DAWNZERA really be able to outstrip market leaders TAKHZYRO and ORLADEYO; if so, how long will it take?

Already approved therapies are exciting but expected new 2026 approvals are the big story.

General

TRYNGOLZA in treatment of FCS and DAWNZERA in treatment of HAE are sure to add an entirely new dimension to Ionis' minuscule product revenues. However upcoming approvals expected later in 2026 will seal the ongoing significance of product revenues for Ionis if approved.

Presentation slide 18 below listing expected 2026 approvals opens the door to Ionis' first wholly owned blockbuster drug:

TRYNGOLZA in treatment of sHTG.

The real potential excitement will come as Ionis' sNDA for TRYNGOLZA in treatment of sHTG works its way through the FDA. In this regard Ionis'01/12/2026 press release noted:

  • Potential approval and launch of olezarsen as the new standard of care for severe hypertriglyceridemia (sHTG)
    • Submitted U.S. Supplemental New Drug Application (sNDA), following receipt of Breakthrough Therapy Designation
      • Olezarsen achieved a highly statistically significant placebo-adjusted reduction of up to 72% in fasting triglycerides and an 85% reduction in acute pancreatitis events with favorable safety and tolerability in pivotal Phase 3 CORE and CORE2 studies
    • Increasing annual olezarsen peak net sales guidance to >$2 billion from >$1 billion based on strong product profile and positive Phase 3 data

TRYNGOLZA's FDA approval in treatment of FCS without any black box warning and with a minimum of other safety concerns on its label would seem to bode well for FDA approval to treat the wider universe of sHTG patients.

Zilganersen in treatment of Alexander disease

In its 12/02/2025 release Ionis announced topline results from its pivotal Phase 1-3 study (NCT04849741) of zilganersen treating patients with Alexander disease (AxD). According to the release:

...zilganersen 50 mg demonstrated statistically significant and clinically meaningful stabilization on the primary endpoint of gait speed as assessed by the 10-Meter Walk Test (10MWT) compared to control at week 61 (mean difference 33.3%, p=0.0412) with favorable safety and tolerability. Zilganersen also demonstrated consistent benefit in key secondary endpoints.

In response to these successful results the FDA awarded zilganersen Breakthrough Therapy designation in the treatment of AxD. AxD ia vile, ultrarare, progressive and often fatal genetic neurological diseases. There are only ~500 reported cases. This sparsity of reported cases limits its peak revenue potential. William Blair pegs this at $295 million.

Bepirovirsen in treatment of Chronic Hepatitis B

Back in 08/2019 GSK [GSK] exercised its option to license Ionis' antisense medicines for people with chronic hepatitis B virus (CHB) infection; the license called for milestone payments up to $262 million, and tiered royalties in the low double digits on net sales.

Fast forward to today and GSK is predicting that its Ionis licensed bepirovirsen will generate >2 billion pounds ($2.70 billion) in peak annual sales. This should unlock remaining unpaid milestones and generate significant royalties in the range of 10-12%.

Of course there are many potential slips between cup and lip, not the least of which being regulatory approvals around the world; however Bepirovirsen should provide an ongoing stream of nice revenues with its possible 2026 launch per slide 24 of the Presentation.

ionis commitment to strong liquidity remains intact.

I had to check my always fallible recollection that Ionis tends to maintain year end cash balances in the range of $1.7-$2.0+ billion. I spot checked Q4 earnings calls back to 2020 and confirmed such was the case. Its latest available earnings Call as I write on 01/18/2026 is for Q3/2025 at which CFO Hougen reported:

...we now expect to end the year with a cash balance of more than $2.1 billion, highlighting our strong balance sheet that will support continued investment to drive accelerating growth.

Its cash burn as reflected by its Q3/2025 10-Q net cash used in operations for the nine months ending 9/30/2025 is ~$131 million or ~$15 million a month. This was down from its 9 month cash burn in 2024 of ~$385 million or ~$43 million a month. Either way its cash runway extends at least ~4 years, well beyond expected 2028 cash flow positive as shown by Presentation slide 20.

Conclusion

With its abundance of growth drivers, I continue to rate Ionis as a "Strong Buy". While I am very much an Ionis bull. It would be wrong to assume hat it is without risk. I have noticed that my biotech portfolio as a whole has been struggling since the start of 2026.

Overall Ionis has lost nearly $7 since its high of ~$83 on 01/07/2026 as shown by the chart below. Those of a bearish cast may draw comfort that its downtrend continued in spite of Ionis' positive presentation at the J.P. Morgan Conference on 01/13/2026.

data.title: FDT: Superior Fundamentals And Returns With High Volatility
data.description:
Strategy

First Trust Developed Markets Ex-US AlphaDEX® Fund ETF (FDT) was launched on 4/18/2011 and tracks the Nasdaq AlphaDEX® Developed Markets Ex-US™ Index. FDT has a portfolio of 300 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.68%, a 12-month trailing yield of 3.27% and an expense ratio of 0.80%. Distributions are paid quarterly.

As described by First Trust, the methodology starts from the Nasdaq Developed Markets Ex-US™ Index. Stocks are ranked separately on growth and value factors. A stock’s “selection score” is defined as the best of its two style ranks. The top 300 stocks based on the selection score are divided into quintiles based on their scores. The top quintiles receive a higher weight and the stocks are equally weighted within each quintile. Finally, index constituents and weights are adjusted to meet country and sector weight constraints.

In summary, FDT has a “value or growth” strategy.

The index is rebalanced semi-annually and the fund’s turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 97%. I will use as a benchmark an ex-U.S. developed markets index, represented by iShares Core MSCI International Developed Markets ETF (IDEV).

Portfolio

About 58% of asset value is invested in large and mega-caps and 42% in mid-caps, with a focus on Asia (55%). The heaviest country in the portfolio is Japan (28.9%), followed by South Korea (15.5%) and Canada (12.4%). Other countries are below 7%. The weight of Hong Kong is 6.4%, representing a moderate exposure to Chinese equities.

The portfolio has a focus on industrials (29.7%) and notable exposure in materials (18%) and consumer discretionary (14%). Others sectors are below 11%. Compared to the benchmark, FDT significantly overweights the top three sectors listed above, and mostly downplays financials.

The portfolio is highly diversified, with very low company-specific risk. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 7.9% of asset value and the heaviest position weighs only 1%.

Takeaway

First Trust Developed Markets Ex-US AlphaDEX® Fund ETF (FDT) has a portfolio of 300 stocks with either value or growth characteristics. FDT has strong fundamentals, dividend growth and diversification, with a focus on Japan and industrials. FDT has outperformed the benchmark and a number competitors in total return, but lags them in Sharpe ratio due to high volatility. FDT is well-suited for international diversification, although risk-averse investors may prefer IQLT or GSIE, which offer better risk-benefit ratios and lower fees.

data.title: Rigetti Computing: Too Much Hype, Waiting For Better Entry
data.description:
Rigetti Computing (RGTI) is one of the several pure-play public companies focusing on the quantum technology space. This area of the market has received high levels of attention over the past 18+ months, with the narrative constantly moving between bulls and bears. One thing is certain, the heightened volatility provides investors many opportunities, both on the long and short side.

Rigetti's approach to quantum is based on superconducting qubits, which are designed, built, and fabricated all by Rigetti. In quantum, the technological approach is truly a competitive differentiator. Companies such a as Rigetti, D-Wave (QBTS), Quantum Computing (QUBT), and IonQ (IONQ) are all taking different approaches to achieve quantum computing advancements. In my opinion, it has yet to be seen which method will garner the most traction and be the most effective, which is why volatility across this sector exists.

The chart above does a great job showing how much hype has been pulled into the stock over the past 1-2 years. With many emerging technologies, investors are often willing to pay aggressive multiple well before the technological breakthroughs. Rigetti is no outlier here, investors are currently paying a high premium with hopes of seeing the technological breakthrough down the road.

Yes, for those quantum companies that experience success, there will be massive amounts of upside from current levels. However, one needs to weigh the likelihood of success with what the stock is currently pricing in. In my opinion, Rigetti's stock is still pricing in a very high degree of success, despite some recent challenges with the product timeline - more on that below.

For now, I believe investors should wait for a better entry point. As alluded to above, the high levels of volatility have created several good entry and exit points. With too much hype currently embedded in shares, the upcoming quarter will likely induce some volatile stock action, potentially setting up a more attractive entry point in a few weeks.

Improving Momentum, But Some Challenges

When it comes to quantum, and I am no expert in this domain, but two of the key focuses for investors is the competitive differentiation and the product roadmap. Given how early the industry is in developing fully functional quantum machines, there is much debate around the most efficient approach.

For Rigetti, they are taking an approach that focuses on superconducting quibits, which is pretty similar to the methods IBM and Google are taking. What makes Rigetti an interesting play is its end-to-end infrastructure management, something that is differentiated from the other pure-play competitors.

As seen in the chart above, Rigetti is building out a variety of capabilities across the ecosystem, rather than solely focusing on one area. At the heart of the company is its chiplet-based scaling. This has led to Rigetti deploying 18 quantum systems to-date, and while the revenue scale is clearly not there, this installed base may provide significant long-term opportunities. With breakthrough technologies, one of the most important aspects is being first to market. Yes, scaling revenue is the ultimate goal, but it is critical for emerging technologies to develop a customer base to trial, use, and eventually scale the technology. Monetization is often a second-hand thought, and this may remain the case for a few more years.

As mentioned above, Rigetti's approach is quasi-similar to IBM and Google. This does give a little more validation to the chiplet-based scaling methodology, but competing against these giants is no easy task. In my opinion, this does but Rigetti at a slight disadvantage given the financial scale and prowess of IBM and Google. However, I do believe this presents a call option on shares. Given Rigetti's end-to-end approach, this would make for an attractive acquisition if share weakness were to persist.

The other most important part when looking at these quantum names is the product roadmap. Is it going to be perfect? No. Should is reasonably represent how quickly the technology can ramp. Yes.

Rigetti has set several product and tech-related targets that they will need to grow into. For example, the company expects they will ramp to 1,000+ qubits by 2027 with the 2-qubit gate fidelity ramp reaching 99.8% during the same timeframe.

During the company's recent earnings call, management discussed the trajectory of achieving these targets, and sounded confident in their ability.

On the technology front, we remain on track to deliver our 100-plus qubit chiplet-based quantum system with an anticipated 99.5% median 2-qubit gate fidelity by the end of 2025. I'm also excited to share our 2026, 2027 road map updates. We expect to deploy a 150-plus qubit system by or around the end of 2026, with an anticipated 99.7% median 2-qubit gate fidelity. And by or around the end of 2027, we expect to deploy a 1,000-plus qubit system with an anticipated 99.8% median 2-qubit gate fidelity.

Achieving these targets would be a giant step forward, but that is not the end goal. Rigetti will need several more years of development before they are truly ready to deploy large-scale quantum compute. The chart above even shows the timeline going out to at least 2030 as further error correction happens in conjunction with broad quantum applications.

What worries me the most is that delays to these timelines could potentially have material impacts to its competitive positioning and investor sentiment. Though the goal was to achieve 100+ quibits with a 99.5% median 2-quibit gate fidelity by the end of 2025, this goal was pushed out a quarter.

While we are making strong progress with Cepheus-1-108Q, we are taking more time to test and optimize the system to ensure the performance is up to our standards.

We have a clear understanding of what we need to do to achieve 99.5% median two-qubit gate fidelity, and are well on our way. Building higher qubit count systems can uncover new challenges. We encountered complexities with our tunable couplers and have decided to run another iteration of our chip to further optimize the system and improve fidelity.

A one quarter delay should not be a major impediment, nor should it be ignored. Getting this technology correct is paramount and this will take time. However, if Rigetti were to see ongoing challenges around accomplishing its stated targets, I believe this could turn into a major cause for concern.

Financial Review

As alluded to a few times, broad commercialization is the end-goal, not the immediate target. Thus, the quarterly updates are helpful for the company to disclose additional projects, collaborations, and liquidity positioning.

Revenue during the past quarter was just $1.9 million and is a poor indicator of the underlying health of the business. At some point, there will be a significant inflection in revenue and profitability, but estimates are more likely to overstate how quick this can be achieved. For now, my focus is on the pipeline of deals and liquidity.

On the commercial side, there were some positive updates this past quarter, such as a purchase order for two quantum systems, totaling ~$5.7 million. Delivery for both systems is expected to occur in the first half of 2026. Additionally, Rigetti announced a three-year $5.8 million contract with the U.S. Air Force Research Lab, which will focus on advancing the superconducting quantum network.

From a liquidity perspective, Rigetti had ~$600 million of cash/investments at the end of the quarter, with the 9-month FCF burn of ~$60 million, which equates to roughly $80 million annual burn rate. This gives the company a good amount of financial flexibility for the foreseeable future. However, if accelerated investments were required to speed up advancements to achieve the roadmap targets, this could cause Rigetti to tap into the debt/equity markets.

At the end of the day, liquidity and balance sheet strength are key to success. Rigetti has a strong balance sheet and enough liquidity to keep investing.

Valuation

With broad-based commercialization still a few years away, valuation is much more focused on technological progress and commercial partners. Revenue is essentially immaterial relative to the company's ~$8.5 billion market cap. As seen in the chart below, all of the pure-play quantum companies trade at sky-high revenue multiples.

The combination of high stock prices, partially supported by excess investor optimism, and low revenue values results in these pricey multiples. Of course, no rational investors is solely looking at Rigetti's revenue base as the determining factor for valuation.

One needs to look at the product roadmap, technological progress, and ecosystem partners in order to embed some premium in the stock. Right now, I believe investors are pricing in a high degree of success for Rigetti relative to the likely outcome. That is not to say Rigetti will be unsuccessful, rather, I believe there is an unfavorable risk-reward with the current stock price.

Risks

The biggest risk to my bearish thesis on Rigetti revolves around the company's technological breakthroughs. If the company achieves "success" earlier than its product roadmap suggest, investors may start to view Rigetti as the early winner in this market. Having a first-mover advantage is huge, and investors would likely pay a nice premium to own the perceived winner.

Additionally, if Rigetti were to start signing material contracts, that could indicative of future success. An earlier-than-expected revenue ramp would go a long way in quelling some of the bear thesis.

Finally, there is the optionality of IBM, Google, or another large technology company to acquire Rigetti. Given the similarities between Rigetti's quibit approach to IBM and Google, these two seem like the logical acquirers. Given their financial resources, a takeout premium could be large.

Final Thoughts

I remain bullish on the long-term outlook for the quantum industry. One only needs to look at the recent bipartisan quantum bill that was introduced to get a sense of how important the quantum initiative is, and will become.

Rigetti is in a good position to be a long-term winner. However, the current stock price is reflective of too high of a probability of success. Yes, the company has outlined a clear, and ambitious product roadmap. However, a recent delay in achieving its 2025 targets is a bit of a yellow flag and something that will need to be monitored.

For now, I believe investors would be better off waiting for another pullback before building a position in the name. With the company's next quarter report coming in just a few weeks, management will provide insight into how they expect 2026 to evolve. Given the volatility in this name, I believe there is more downside risk here.
